speaker: interviewer
text: Before you continue with your having gone to Bergen-Belsen, I'd like you to tell me how many of your brothers and sisters ultimately survived the war. You were with two of your brothers there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with two of my brothers. All three of us survived. Benjamin, Schmuel Meir, we survived Buchenwald together. We learned later, months later, that the brother who lived in Germany before the war--

speaker: interviewer
text: David.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Baruch. That he-- this was, oh, I would say, two years later we learned that he is in England, that he went from Germany to England. When we got in touch with some cousins in Canada, we learned that he is alive and he finds himself in London, England. But otherwise, the three of them, yes. And my sister in Brazil survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Yohavet. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when had she gone--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Yohavet. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: When had she gone to--

speaker: interviewee
text: She went in 1935 to Brazil. So, I mean, she wasn't affected by the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: But everybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody else perished. Yeah. They and their children. Matter of fact, one of my brother's children's son was in Buchenwald but came in January from Auschwitz. And then, they sent him away from Buchenwald. And he died on the way. One of my-- another brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother of mine, also with the transport from Auschwitz to Buchenwald, died on the way. [? Aaron, ?] he died also. His wife survived, because he was married. He was married. His wife survived, I saw after the war. And finally, she remarried and settled in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister-in-law's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my sister-in-law's name. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went with your two brothers to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went from Buchenwald. After the Russians takeover the Republic of Weimar, so we want to Buchenwald. Over there, we met some people from my hometown. One of them, [? Yatcha ?] [? Steinman ?], came, because he heard his wife survived in Bergen-Belsen. So he came there with another friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we met them. And, you know, we were talking about where we're going to settle. He said, why don't you come with me? That he was liberated near Regensburg and worked on Donau. And he said, why don't you come with us? And we'll stay there for awhile. So after a few weeks, we went down to Regensburg. And we met with them. And then they found a-- we stayed with them a couple of days. And then we're given a place where to stay. We took over, like, somebody's house. Had his wife survived? Did he find her?

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife survived, yes, from before the war as well. And we settled and worked on the Donau. In December of that year, a friend of mine who was living in Frankfort am Main, Salzheim near Frankfort am Main, came to visit his uncle there. And he said to me, what are you doing with those people? Because all those people were a lot older than me. He said, why don't you come with me? He was in a kibbutz, yehud in Salzheim.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I decided. I left my brothers, and I went to the kibbutz in Frankfurt. And I was in Frankfurt in the kibbutz a few months. He got married. My friend got married in that kibbutz. And eventually, he went to Israel with his wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I left the kibbutz and went in to live in the DP camp in Salzheim by Frankfurt am Main. And I remained in that DP camp, till I left for Canada. I worked for the UNRRA police, for the camp police, DP police, as a policeman in Salzheim. I went to Stuttgart to go through a course for police duty which they taught us. I think it was two weeks or three weeks. I don't remember. I got a certificate finished DP police for the UNRRA. And I was there until I left for Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever consider going back to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never considered going back from Poland, because I was at home when everybody was sent away, if you remember. And I knew there was no house left. It was burned down. And I knew all my family went away. And if there was any chance of anybody living, I would have known. And there was no use going back.

speaker: interviewer
text: When had your house been burnt down?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right after they burned the city. The Germans, right after we were-- whoever-- you know, the street I lived on was burned down, so there was no use going back there. There was nothing for me to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your two brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing. We all were together.

speaker: interviewer
text: They came to Salzheim?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My older brother, Schmuel Meir, his girlfriend from before he war-- and he went-- he found afterwards some burning building, so he traveled back and brought her there. And they got married and worked on the Donau. My other brother, Benjamin, came to Salzheim, and he lived with me. Finally, he was in a hold up in 1948. Just a month before we were supposed to leave for Canada, we had a hold up in the house. And he was killed. I mean, he was shot. And three days later, he died in Salzheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was one Yugoslavian, I think, and one Ukrainian. I even met those people. We did some business with them, black market business. And they came that day. And they're supposed to have 100 gold pieces, Czech gold pieces for sale. We didn't have all that money to do that kind of business. We had some other people. There was seven or eight people at the table. When those two guys came in-- they came in the afternoon. They left some stuff in the house, and they went out. And then they came back in the evening. When they set down to the table, after they counted out their first 10 pieces, they said they want money. So they gave them dollars, you know, for their gold pieces. As they were handing them the money they got up with two guns, start blazing away. I was shot through my leg, but only it didn't hit my bone. It just went through [INAUDIBLE]. My brother had seven bullets in him. So another man, you know, he was operated and his foot had to be shortened. Those things. And a year later, they caught those people, you know, the police, they caught him and friends. And they were deported into their home countries, because they couldn't sentence them if they chose to go back to Ukraine or go back to Yugoslavia. I mean, they couldn't hold them in Germany. And that was the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide to emigrate to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found relatives. I mean, I personally didn't know them, because they left Opatow before I was born. But one of the-- a member of Parliament, Mr. [? Zeltzberg, ?] came to visit the camp. And while we're talking, I told him my name. And I said, I think I had some relatives from before the war in Canada and gave him the name. And he said, I know them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the next day, I went with him up to the Canadian Council and made an application. He notified my cousins in Toronto. And they sent the papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the easiest way to get away from there, because I was sitting for six months on my [YIDDISH] or on my suitcases. We wanted go with Aliyah Bet to Israel and it didn't work out. You didn't want to sit in the camp. You wanted to go away and start a new life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your other brother go to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: My one brother who was killed, he was supposed to leave with me together to Canada. And the other brother went with his wife to New York, because she had relatives in New York. And they sent them papers to go to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you had a chance, either before the war or after the war or even during the war, to have a girlfriend of your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me? No. No, I was too young for that. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And you know, you had friends. I mean, I had friends. Like, I met my wife through a friend. Her father was the president of the camp, Skarzysko. I was there. After the war, when I was in Salzheim, I saw that girl. Her name was [? Sarenka. ?] And her father saw me. He remembered me from camp, because I used to-- they lived this side of the barrack, and I lived in this side of the barrack. We used to see each other. He said his daughter's there in Salzheim. So I went to visit her. And that's when I met my wife, because the two of them were going to school in Salzheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your wife's name at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dorothy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Devorah Kirszenbaum. And they were going to-- started taking Hebrew lessons. And I helped them with their Hebrew, because I learned Hebrew before the war. I finished [NON-ENGLISH] school in Poland, '44. And that's how we met. I mean, we're still in contact with the girlfriend. She lives in New York. You know, still in contact with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you two develop a romance before you went to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then, you know, I left in end of April. And then I arrived in-- I made it first to Toronto. Dorothy came in November with a group of children, young children, to Canada. And she also was in Toronto right away. I mean, she didn't have anybody else in Canada. And she came November, '48. And we got married March, '49, March 20th.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of work did you do in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked as a cutter in the schmatta business, the needle trade. That's what I learned. I didn't have no profession. A cousin of mine knew some people in the union, the trade, so he got me a job. I worked very hard. I worked as a shipper. That's how I started. Cleaned the floor. Worked as a shipper and tried to learn in my spare time at the table what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I worked for the same firm, I worked for seven years. After seven years, I said, Mr. [? Worsh, ?] I said, the Bible says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I says, six years you should work. Seventh year, you should go free. That was in the [NON-ENGLISH], in the Torah, you know? A slave only works so many years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I left and I went and I worked in another job. But then, you know, my wife became a teacher in Canada. We had two children. And she--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter's name is Gloria. My son's name is Joey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they named for? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Gloria's named after my wife's mother and my mother. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The same-- my son is named after my father and her father. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when, you know, she was teaching she had 40 kids in the class. And came wintertime-- and in Canada, you have snow. Winter, you have to take off the boots and put them on. You know, they go out for recess. So we decided, if you have to work, we might as well work in a nice climate. We decided to come to California.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1962. We even forgot about it. There was a man, you know, where I made the application. I didn't have-- I mean, my wife had sisters in New York and one in St. Paul, Minnesota.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when we made the applications, there was a man from my hometown-- he was before the war-- in Toronto. His name was [? Anshel Weiss. ?] And he was a travel agent. The people who had to send money to Poland, you know, it went through him. And anybody wanted to bring his relatives he arranged all those things, you know? So we went to him and made the application.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years later, three years later, we saw him at a bar mitzvah. He said, you know, your papers are going to come through. He said, you've got to make up your mind if you want to leave or you want to stay here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, no, we're going to leave, because we didn't have relatives here. I wasn't out here before. I didn't come out here to investigate. I just decided to come out, and that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came out. A friend of mine who lived in-- we knew each other in labor camp-- I mean, in DP camp after the war-- lived here for two, three years already. So he waited at the airport for me. Came out. A few days later, I rented a place and start looking for a job. And I landed a job here with the most famous designer at that time in the United States, Rudi Gernreich. He came out with the topless, with the chemise. You know, he won three Coty awards at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I worked for as long as he was in business. For seven years I worked for him, from January, '63 till, I think, about '69.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I worked downtown. Also a man I met. He came after the war here from Chicago-- he came to Chicago. Lithuanian fella. And then, he came out here. He opened a place. I knew him, so I went to work for him. And I worked till my retirement.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you retire?

speaker: interviewee
text: I retired a few years ago, when I was 65. Right now, I'm close to 70 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have your children grown and gotten married?

speaker: interviewee
text: My children-- my son's bar mitzvah was in [INAUDIBLE] in 1969. My daughter graduated from Grant High School. Also, my son graduated [INAUDIBLE] high school. My daughter was the homecoming queen, The Cotton Days Queen, Outstanding Student Award, the first girl to be elected President of Commencement Address. She delivered the commencement address in the high school graduation. Then she went for two years to UCSD in La Jolla. After that, she went overseas to Jerusalem, University of Jerusalem. And in 1971, the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: If we could just move ahead, because I have a few other questions. Does your daughter have any children now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their--

speaker: interviewee
text: She has two kids. She has a daughter, Lisa Beth, which is 16 years old. She has a son, Ned, which is 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son has got three children. He's got-- his oldest son is Jonathan, which is nine. His next one is Jeffrey, which is going to be eight. And the third one is Daniel, which is going to be six this month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, looking back on all that you went through, how is it that you think you were able to survive what so many people didn't? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You can't-- one person will tell you faith, another one tell you luck. Third person, you're in the right time at the right place. So you can't attribute everything to what happened. I mean, it's like something pushed me to go-- I mean, I was picked out from the first hundred, then the city-- and then we went to Sandomierz. Same thing happened. They took me out to another camp. I was in labor camp. I worked. I was in the other labor camp. I mean, I was strong. I was healthy. I was young. I mean, I had a good physique. Work didn't bother me, much. And I have to attribute that, that I was strong and healthy. That's what I got slapped in Buchenwald the first evening, because I looked so healthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I know you have some photographs that you want to share with us. But before we look at those, is there any final message that you'd like to give to the people who will be seeing this tape in the future? Anything you would like to add to what you have said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I say, what the Bible said about you should never forget what Amalek wanted to do to you. We should never forget what the Germans wanted to do us. I mean, you can't hold responsible every one of them today what happened 50 years ago. But still, it was their culture, their education, what they tried to do, you know, a certain group of people in their country tried to do at that time to the world. Not only to the Jews, they did it to the gypsies. They did it to the Russians. There was 40 million Russians killed, farmers. You know, when they invaded, the roads was open for them, the Ukrainian and the Russians welcomed them. But then when they started burning and torturing all those people, that's when the resistance became. See, at the time we were the underclass. We were the scum of the people. They were the, you know, chosen race. So this, we shouldn't forget, what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you, very much. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my father and my brother. It was taken 1933 when my father was visiting my brother in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schmuel. I mean Baruch. And this is-- and next to it, is my mother's picture, which was cut out from another picture nearby.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the picture of her taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this one, she was still a girl, I think. Young girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the late 1800s?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was in the 1900 that she was a girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Yosef [INAUDIBLE]. And my brother's name here is Baruch. And my mother's name is Esther [INAUDIBLE]. This is a picture taken the day my sister left for Brazil, which is behind my father, [INAUDIBLE]. To the left, starting the left, is my brother, Schmuel. Next to him is my sister-in-law, [INAUDIBLE]. In the front is Schmuel's daughter, [INAUDIBLE]. Next is my oldest brother, David. Next is mine the brother's daughter, [INAUDIBLE]. And then is my sister, [INAUDIBLE]. My father in the middle. The little girl's [INAUDIBLE]. Also a child of Schmuel. Next is my sister, [INAUDIBLE]. And then my brother, [INAUDIBLE]. And my sister-in-law, Schmuel's wife. Next, that's me when I was eight years old. And taken in 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Opatow.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Part of Kielce, Poland. Me. This is me on the right side. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture was taken in July, 1945, in Hanover. You know, as an identification to be able to get ration cards, you know, while you travel through Germany. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1945, I was 20 years old. This picture is me and my older brother, Schmuel Meir. This was taken, I think, in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is which?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't take the--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, which one is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the left is me. And next to me is my brother, Schmuel. This is my brother, Benjamin, which was killed in Germany in 1948, before we was supposed to both of us leave for Canada. This is me, Allen Greenstein, the day we got married, and my wife Dorothy, taken in March the 20th, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Toronto, Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, that isn't [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my daughter, Gloria, and her husband, David Eiseman. They were married in 1976. December the 25th, in 1976.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New Jersey. Englewood, New Jersey. That's my son, Joseph, and his wife, Lisa. They were married in Israel, in 1984. That's my granddaughter, Lisa. She is 16 years old. She lives in Riverdale, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is her mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her mother is my daughter, [? Laura ?] Greenstein. That's my grandson, Eddie Eiseman. The picture was taken in 1992. He is 12 years old. That's my grandson, Jonathan. He's nine years old. It's my son's oldest child. He lives in Miami.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my second grandchild from my son. His name is Jeffrey. He is going to be eight years old. That's my youngest grandson from my son. He's going to be six years old, August the 16th.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Daniel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you, very much.
